findings.description: Docstring promises strict YYYY-MM-DD parsing, but CPython strptime's %m/%d accept non-zero-padded values (e.g. '2024-1-2' parses successfully), so the 'strictly' contract is violated; date.fromisoformat would actually be strict.
Module docstring claims 'pure functions' but utc_now (and humanize_delta's default reference) read the system clock, so the purity claim is inaccurate.

Because datetime subclasses date, passing datetimes type-checks and yields datetime elements with (end-start).days floor-truncating fractional spans, which can silently drop the final calendar day from the 'inclusive' range.
day_range materializes the entire span as a list (millions of objects for multi-millennium spans); yielding lazily would bound memory for large ranges.
business_days_between iterates every day in the span (O(n) time plus the full day_range list) when a closed-form O(1) weekday-count formula exists.
The awareness guard only checks tzinfo is None, so a pseudo-aware datetime whose tzinfo.utcoffset() returns None slips past and the subtraction raises TypeError instead of the intended ValueError.
int() truncates toward zero, so a moment less than one second in the future yields seconds == 0 and reports 'just now' instead of 'in the future'.
The scale tops out at weeks, so old timestamps render as e.g. '104 w ago' with no month/year tiers, which is unusual output for a humanizer even granting the documented coarseness.
The divmod remainders reassigned into seconds/minutes/hours/days are dead stores never read afterward, which linters will flag as unused values.
is_weekend is the only public function without a docstring, inconsistent with the rest of the module.
The name business_days_between implies holiday awareness while the implementation only excludes weekends; the docstring clarifies this but the name can mislead callers.
Mixing naive and aware datetimes in clamp_to_range raises a bare TypeError from comparison, inconsistent with humanize_delta's explicit ValueError guard and undocumented in the docstring.
humanize_delta's docstring omits the ValueError it raises for non-aware inputs, while parse_iso_date documents its raising behavior.
